summary: Cathy O'Neil announces she is writing a book called 'Weapons of Math Destruction' for Random House, working with editor Amanda Cook. She describes the book proposal process with her agent Jay Mandel, noting that Crown Publishing made a pre-emptive offer before a bidding war could develop. The book will argue that mathematical modeling has become a destructive force across finance, education, medicine, politics, and the workplace, exacerbating inequality and endangering democracy. She solicits reader input on examples of problematic models and feedback loops to include in the book.
key_points: The book will examine how mathematical models have become pervasive and destructive across multiple sectors of society
Key themes include feedback loops, perverted incentives, and 'creepy modeling' that exacerbate inequality
Value Added Models for teachers and Value-at-Risk in finance are already confirmed as examples
O'Neil is actively crowdsourcing examples from readers to ensure comprehensive coverage
The book builds on her unique background as a Harvard math PhD, former Wall Street quant, and Occupy movement advisor
money_quotes: Mathematical modeling has become a pervasive and destructive force in society—in finance, education, medicine, politics, and the workplace
Current models exacerbate inequality and endanger democracy
I'd love my readers to weigh in on crucial examples of models and industries that you think might get overlooked
Your favorite example of a family of models... that is illustrative of feedback loops, or perverted incentives, or creepy modeling
key_insight: Mathematical models, despite their veneer of objectivity, can become weapons that entrench inequality and threaten democratic institutions when deployed without accountability.
